skill.name: web-design-guidelines
skill.description: Review UI files against the Vercel Web Interface Guidelines and report design, accessibility, and UX issues.
skill.summary: Review web UI code against current interface guidelines.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static shell execution and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and ordinary descriptive text in SKILL.md. The skill does require fetching remote guideline content and then following those fetched output instructions, which creates a prompt-injection and supply-chain risk if the remote source changes.

medium_findings.title: Remote Guideline Content Followed at Runtime
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md instructs the assistant to fetch a hardcoded remote URL and use the fetched content for rules and output format instructions. This is legitimate for a live guideline reviewer, but remote content could change or include prompt-injection text.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network dependency is explicit, and line 26 tells the assistant to rely on fetched instructions. There is no evidence of malicious intent in the local file, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the backticks appear inside Markdown text and a fenced URL block. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command invocation is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown formatting only. They do not contain executable code or an instruction to run a local command.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography on descriptive Markdown lines, including the skill name and usage text. No hash function, cipher call, cryptographic API, or credential handling appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter and usage prose, not cryptographic code. I found no evidence of weak algorithms being used.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded Remote Fetch Source
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill points to a raw GitHub URL as the authoritative guideline source. This is a controlled public source, but it still creates dependency on external content at review time.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is plainly present and line 26 instructs use of WebFetch. The risk depends on future remote content, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

content.user_title: Audit Web UI Against Design Guidelines
content.value_statement: Web interfaces often drift from accessibility and usability standards during fast frontend work. This skill fetches current interface guidelines and applies them to selected files.
actual_capabilities: Fetches the current Web Interface Guidelines from the configured source URL.
Reviews user-selected UI files or file patterns.
Checks frontend work for design, accessibility, and UX guideline issues.
Reports findings in a concise file and line oriented format.
Prompts for target files when the user does not provide a path.
limitations: Requires network access to fetch the latest guideline document.
Depends on the accuracy and safety of the remote guideline source.
Reviews code and markup, but does not replace browser-based visual testing.
Does not automatically fix the issues it reports.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge UI Review
use_cases.description: Review changed frontend files before merging a pull request.

use_cases.title: Accessibility Triage
use_cases.description: Find likely accessibility and interaction issues in pages or components.

use_cases.title: Design System Compliance
use_cases.description: Check implementation details against shared interface standards.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-design-guidelines to audit app/settings/page.tsx for accessibility and UX issues.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Feature Folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Run web-design-guidelines on src/features/billing/**/* and report only actionable findings.

prompt_templates.title: Review Before Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use web-design-guidelines on the changed UI files in this branch and prioritize release-blocking issues.

output_examples.input: Review a navigation component for guideline compliance.
output_examples.output: The result highlights specific file and line findings for spacing, focus states, labels, and responsive behavior.

output_examples.input: Audit a settings page before release.
output_examples.output: The result lists concise issues with enough context for an engineer to locate and fix each problem.

output_examples.input: Check a feature folder for accessibility risks.
output_examples.output: The result groups actionable UI concerns found across the selected files without producing implementation patches.

best_practices: Review small file sets so findings stay specific and actionable.
Treat fetched guideline text as reference material, not as higher-priority system instructions.
Confirm important findings in a browser or accessibility testing tool.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill as the only release approval step.
Do not let remote guideline text override security or repository instructions.
Do not run broad audits without giving the skill clear file targets.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews selected UI files against the Web Interface Guidelines and reports likely design, accessibility, and UX issues.

faq.question: Does it modify my code?
faq.answer: No. The skill is written to report findings, not to edit files.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. It fetches the latest guideline document from a raw GitHub URL.

faq.question: Can it review an entire app?
faq.answer: It can review file patterns, but smaller scopes usually produce clearer findings.

faq.question: Is this a full accessibility audit?
faq.answer: No. It can identify likely issues, but manual testing and automated accessibility tools are still needed.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
